metadata.file_role: Puffballs, jellies, stinkhorns, and unusual forms
metadata.purpose: Mushrooms with unusual fertile surfaces or fruitbody forms not suited to the main gilled, bolete, polypore, or ascomycete groups.
metadata.organization_rule: Physical file placement follows stable biological or morphological identity. Useful parts, routes, safety, and seasonal behavior remain record-level fields.

records.notes: Only edible when the interior is pure white and uniform all the way through.
records.culinary_uses: Good edible only while the interior is pure white and uniform. Slice open every specimen; discard any yellowing, browning, developing gills, or odd structure.
records.general_notes: A cut-open check is mandatory because immature deadly amanitas can fool people when still buttoned.
records.edibility_detail: Good edible when young and solid white inside.

mushroom_profile.spore_print: No conventional cap-on-paper print; the internal gleba changes from white to olive-yellow and eventually brownish spore dust.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer into fall in open ground and edges.
flesh_color: Pure white and uniform when immature
Olive-yellow to brownish as spores mature

records.field_identification: Large white ball-like puffball with no developing cap, stem, gills, or internal outline when cut open. The interior must be solid, featureless, and pure white; yellow, olive, brown, purple, black, marbled, gelatinous, or structured interiors are reject signs.
records.mushroom_card_note: Good edible only when cut-open flesh is pure white all the way through.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Calvatia gigantea
use_links.notes: Giant puffball traits and warnings about immature gilled mushrooms/earthballs.

records.look_alike_notes: Slice every puffball vertically. Giant puffballs must be solid white and uniform inside; any developing cap/gills/stem structure points toward an immature gilled mushroom, including deadly Amanita risk. Cut every puffball from top to bottom; eat only specimens with a solid, featureless, pure-white interior, and discard anything showing gills, a stem outline, gelatin, yellowing, browning, or dark flesh. Earthballs and Amanita buttons are the primary danger checks.
records.caution_reason: Interior condition is the safety gate; Amanita buttons and earthballs are the major mistakes.
records.quality_notes: Mild blank-canvas flavor; can be bland or sponge-like unless browned well.
records.eating_use_notes: Slice open every specimen. Eat only when the interior is solid, uniform, marshmallow-white. Brown well.
records.risk_reason: Old puffballs become inedible, and young Amanita buttons or earthballs can be mistaken for puffballs if not cut open.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Old/contaminated puffballs can cause GI upset. Amanita confusion can cause liver/kidney failure. Earthball confusion can cause GI irritation and sometimes neurologic/cardiovascular symptoms.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Round white mushrooms with no visible gills can hide dangerous look-alikes. Edible puffballs must be solid and uniform white inside, with no gills, stem outline, yellow, green, brown, purple, black, or developing cap/stem structure.
records.hard_rule: Cut every puffball from top to bottom. If the inside is not solid pure white with no internal mushroom outline, do not eat it.

lookalike_risk.label: Extreme — Amanita button / earthball confusion possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: Extreme — Amanita button / earthball confusion possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Puffballs are edible only when the interior is solid, featureless, soft, and pure white.
required_checks: Interior must be solid, soft, featureless, and pure white
Reject thick rind, gelatinous layer, yellow/olive/brown/purple/black/marbled/veined interior, or powdery/darkening flesh
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: If the inside is not solid pure white with no internal mushroom outline, do not eat it.

lookalike_traits: Hidden cap, stem, gills, ring, or cup/volva when cut
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Any internal mushroom outline means do not eat as puffball.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / gastrointestinal and possible systemic effects.
lookalike_traits: Dark, purple, brown, black, marbled, veined, powdery, or tough interior
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Dark, marbled, veined, powdery, or tough interiors mean do not eat.

records.source_note: v4.3.209 separates mature internal gleba color from a conventional deposited spore print.

records.food_quality: Good only when young and pure white inside
records.edibility_detail: Edible only when young, firm, and completely white/uniform inside. Slice every puffball-like mushroom top-to-bottom; discard anything with gills, a cap/stem outline, yellow/olive/brown interior, or marbling.
records.culinary_uses: Cook young, pure-white specimens thoroughly. Do not eat old, yellowing, olive, brown, or powdery specimens.
records.overview: Common woodland puffball with short spines or “gems” on the surface.
records.notes: Useful puffball record, but all puffballs require the slice test to avoid immature Amanita or earthball confusion.
records.field_identification: Small to medium terrestrial puffball with whitish surface covered in short spines/warts that can rub off and leave a net-like pattern. Interior must be solid white when edible.
records.habitat: Woods, trail edges, grassy clearings, and forest litter.

flesh_color: Pure white when edible; yellow/olive/brown when too old
mushroom_profile.spore_print: No conventional cap-on-paper print; mature upper gleba and spore powder are olive-brown and are released through an apical pore by raindrops and wind.
mushroom_profile.hard_rule: Slice every puffball-like mushroom; eat only if the interior is completely white and uniform.

use_links.notes: Gem-studded puffball morphology, internal gleba maturation, and apical-pore spore release.

records.mushroom_card_note: Spiny woodland puffball; slice every specimen and eat only if pure white inside.

records.food_quality: Edible young only when pure white inside
records.edibility_detail: Edible only when young and completely white/uniform inside. Slice every specimen. This species commonly grows on decaying wood, which helps separate it from many terrestrial puffballs.
records.culinary_uses: Cook young pure-white specimens thoroughly. Avoid old/yellow/olive/brown or powdery specimens.
records.overview: Small pear-shaped puffball that often fruits in clusters on decaying wood.
records.notes: Important because the wood-growing habit helps distinguish it from some other puffballs, but the same slice-test rule still applies.
records.field_identification: Pear-shaped puffballs in clusters on rotten logs, stumps, buried wood, or wood-rich soil. Interior must be solid white when edible.
records.habitat: Decaying logs, stumps, buried wood, and wood-rich soil.

growth_form: Clustered enclosed puffballs on dead wood
flesh_color: Pure white when edible; yellow/olive/brown when too old
mushroom_profile.spore_print: No conventional cap-on-paper print; internal gleba changes from white to yellowish or olive and eventually brownish spore dust, released through an apical pore by raindrops and wind.
mushroom_profile.hard_rule: Slice every puffball-like mushroom; eat only if the interior is completely white and uniform.

records.mushroom_card_note: Wood-growing puffball; slice every specimen and eat only if pure white inside.

records.notes: A parasitized mushroom turned bright orange-red and dense, usually from a russula or milkcap host.
records.culinary_uses: Choice edible when fully transformed, fresh, firm, and clean. Trim dirty folds, slice to inspect the interior, cook thoroughly, and reject partial, soft, slimy, moldy, sour, purple, or foul specimens.
records.general_notes: Best learned as a transformation fungus rather than a normal cap-and-gill mushroom.
records.edibility_detail: Choice edible when fresh, firm, and fully transformed. This is a parasitic fungus on other mushrooms, usually Russula or Lactarius. Eat only firm, fresh, fully orange specimens with the host mushroom clearly transformed.

underside: Host underside deformed or obscured by parasite crust
Original host gills reduced or buried
smell: mild to seafood-like in some specimens
staining: Orange-red exterior; no diagnostic bruise test
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Not a practical cap-on-paper field test; the host hymenium is overgrown and the parasite produces spores from embedded perithecia
fertile_surface: Orange-red parasite crust with embedded perithecia
mushroom_profile.season_note: Mid-summer through fall; July through September in the app.
host_trees: Host-tree association follows the Russula/Lactarius host
mushroom_profile.summary: Bright orange-red Hypomyces crust thoroughly transforms a Russula, Lactarius, or Lactifluus host into a dense irregular fruitbody with obscured gills.
mushroom_profile.host_certainty: host mushroom group known; exact host often obscured

records.foraging_value: Choice edible when fresh, firm, and fully transformed
records.field_identification: A bright orange to reddish-orange parasitic fungus that covers and deforms a host mushroom, usually a white Russula or Lactarius. Mature lobster mushrooms are firm, dense, irregularly shaped, and covered with a hard, warty or pimply orange crust. The original host’s gills are usually reduced, blunted, or completely obscured.
records.look_alike_notes: Few true look-alikes when fully developed, but partial infections, rotten specimens, and orange moldy mushrooms should not be treated as lobster mushrooms. The whole mushroom should be firm and thoroughly transformed, not just patched with orange mold. Do not eat specimens where the host is still mostly visible, slimy, decaying, or foul-smelling.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject soft, slimy, waterlogged, moldy, sour, insect-riddled, faded, brown, mushy, or foul-smelling specimens. Reject partially parasitized mushrooms where normal gills and host traits remain obvious. Slice open before cooking; interior should be firm and clean, not rotten.
records.image_needs: Needs whole bright orange specimen, warty/pimply crust close-up, obscured/deformed gills, cut pale firm interior, partially infected/reject specimen, and hardwood habitat image.
records.quality_notes: Dense, seafood-like aroma; old specimens become fishy, light, purple, soft, or foul.
records.eating_use_notes: Trim dirty folds, cook well, and use only firm fully parasitized specimens. Dry/powder only sound specimens that are not spoiled.
records.risk_reason: Risk is incomplete parasitization, age/condition, and uncertainty of the host. Half-parasitized forms are not app-safe food candidates.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Mostly gastrointestinal upset risk from spoiled, old, partially parasitized, or uncertain specimens.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: Orange crust should thoroughly cover/deform the host mushroom. Reject specimens where the host is still mostly visible, soft, slimy, purple, foul-smelling, or only patched with orange mold.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat partial, rotten, soft, foul, or mostly untransformed orange-moldy mushrooms as lobster mushrooms.
records.caution_reason: Condition and complete parasitization matter more than the name.

lookalike_risk.label: High — host/partial-infection uncertainty
lookalike_risk.summary: Lobster mushroom is a parasitized Russula/Lactarius-type host. Risk rises with partial infection, rot, mold, or orange-gilled look-alikes.
required_checks: Reject partial orange coating if host traits remain unclear
Reject old, foul, light, purple, soft, or moldy specimens
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Partial orange coating is not enough; if normal host traits are still doing the talking, identify the host separately.

target_traits: Fully transformed hard orange parasite crust
lookalike_traits: White Russula host not fully parasitized
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Partial orange coating is not enough.

lookalike_traits: Milkcap traits still visible, latex present, incomplete parasitism
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If milkcap traits remain, identify the host separately.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: Orange true-gilled clusters from wood are not the same as edible shelf/ridge mushrooms.
target_traits: Target mushroom lacks true orange gills
lookalike_traits: Dense clusters from wood, roots, or buried roots
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If it has true orange gills, route away from the edible target.

use_links.notes: Describes the orange Hypomyces crust, white host interior, Russula/Lactarius hosts, and long food-use history.

use_links.applies_to_part: infected Russula host and Hypomyces parasite
use_links.notes: Documents parasite/host DNA distribution and metabolite changes in wild lobster mushrooms.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Hypomyces lactifluorum
use_links.notes: Orange parasite coating, embedded perithecia, and transformed host morphology.

edible_use.notes: Use only fully transformed, firm, fresh specimens; cut open and reject partial infection, rot, slime, mold, sour odor, or soft interior.

records.source_note: v4.3.209 identifies the orange-red parasite crust and embedded perithecia as the fertile structure; the overgrown host hymenium does not provide a practical cap print.

records.display_name: Shrimp of the woods / aborted Armillaria
records.common_name: Shrimp of the woods / aborted Armillaria
records.notes: The edible target is the firm, pale aborted Armillaria carpophoroid produced when Entoloma abortivum parasitizes Armillaria fruiting tissue.
records.culinary_uses: Use only young, firm, pale aborted forms. Cut open, clean thoroughly, and brown well; the normal gilled Entoloma form is not a beginner food target.
records.edibility_detail: Edible when confidently identified, especially the firm aborted form. The normal gilled form is not recommended for beginners because other Entoloma species can be toxic or inedible. Prefer young, firm, white aborted forms.
records.general_notes: The current verified images show the aborted form and its Armillaria relationship; they are not lobster-mushroom images.

records.foraging_value: Good edible when the firm aborted form is confidently identified
records.field_identification: Best recognized in the aborted form: irregular white to pale tan, popcorn-like or shrimp-like lumps, usually firm and dense, sometimes with pinkish or whitish interior when cut. Often found near decaying wood, roots, stumps, or honey mushrooms. The normal gilled form is grayish to gray-brown with gills that become pinkish as spores mature and should not be a beginner harvest target.
records.look_alike_notes: The aborted form is distinctive, but the normal gilled form can resemble other Entoloma species, some of which are toxic or inedible. Target only the firm white aborted form unless you already know Entoloma identification. Brown-spotted, waterlogged, or soft aborted forms are past prime.
records.bad_condition_warnings: Reject brown-spotted, slimy, waterlogged, soft, bug-riddled, sour-smelling, or rotten aborted forms. Avoid normal gilled forms unless confirmed by an experienced identifier. Do not treat every pale lump near wood as edible; cut and inspect.
records.image_needs: Needs aborted form cluster, cut interior, aborted form near wood/honey mushrooms, normal gilled form comparison, and reject examples showing brown-spotted or waterlogged specimens.

mushroom_profile.underside_type: No normal underside in aborted food form; ordinary gills in normal Entoloma fruitbody
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Pink in the normal gilled Entoloma form; not practical for the aborted food form
taste: not recommended as an ID trait
substrate: Ground near roots, stumps, buried wood, or Armillaria fruiting
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer and fall; August through October in the app.
key_filters: pink print applies only to normal gilled form
mushroom_profile.entry_scope: aborted food form plus normal-form warning
growth_form: Normal gray gilled form as warning comparison
fertile_surface: Absent or deformed in aborted food form
host_trees: Hardwoods and conifers through Armillaria hosts
mushroom_profile.host_certainty: Armillaria association important; exact tree varies
staining: Cut interior white to pinkish white; reject dark or marbled interiors

records.quality_notes: Texture is the main value; flavor can be mild unless browned well. Often dirty.
records.eating_use_notes: Use firm white aborted forms, clean heavily, and brown hard for best flavor.
records.risk_reason: Aborted popcorn-like forms are safer to target; the gilled Entoloma form is much riskier because other Entoloma species can be toxic or inedible.
records.symptoms_body_systems: Gastrointestinal illness is the main concern if confused with toxic Entoloma or eaten in poor condition.
records.field_traits_that_create_risk: The normal gilled form has grayish caps and pinkish gills and should not be a beginner harvest target. Brown-spotted, waterlogged, or soft aborted forms are past prime.
records.hard_rule: Only the firm aborted Armillaria form belongs in the edible basket. Ordinary gray gilled Entoloma-like mushrooms do not.
records.caution_reason: Form matters: aborted form is the food target; gilled Entoloma forms are not beginner-safe.

lookalike_risk.label: High — aborted-form-only edible use
lookalike_risk.summary: The aborted white popcorn/shrimp form is the food target. The normal gilled Entoloma form is a serious ID problem because many Entoloma species are toxic.
required_checks: Expert confirmation required for any gilled Entoloma form
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Only the aborted form belongs in the edible basket.

dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Do not eat ordinary gilled Entoloma-like mushrooms as shrimp of the woods.

target_traits: Irregular lumpy aborted masses near wood/Armillaria areas
lookalike_traits: Round puffball-like mass; must be solid white inside if treated as puffball
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If it looks puffball-like, cut it open and route by interior.
Dark, marbled, or tough interior means do not eat.

use_links.label: Tom Volk, UW–La Crosse: Entoloma abortivum
use_links.notes: Explains the Entoloma–Armillaria relationship and the aborted carpophoroid.

use_links.label: Tom Volk, UW–La Crosse: lobster mushroom and fungal parasitism
use_links.notes: Notes the research conclusion that Entoloma abortivum is the parasite and Armillaria is the aborted host tissue.

edible_use.notes: Use only firm, pale aborted forms; cut open, clean heavily, cook thoroughly, and reject ordinary gilled Entoloma forms.

records.source_note: v4.3.208 corrects the biology and stale image note, adds a source trail, and completes route/spore applicability fields.

records.edibility_detail: Edible when confidently identified and cooked, but this entry is a group-level field record because North American wood-ear names have shifted. Host wood and overall jelly-ear form matter more here than old European names in field guides.
records.culinary_uses: Edible when confidently identified and cooked; valued more for chewy texture than flavor. Use fresh, clean specimens from sound wood, or dry and rehydrate them for soups, stir-fries, and brothy dishes. Avoid old, blackened, polluted, or slimy-decomposing specimens.
records.overview: Wood ears are brown, rubbery-gelatinous fungi that form ear-shaped, cup-shaped, or irregular floppy fruiting bodies on dead wood after wet weather. In North America, older field guides often used European wood-ear names, but current sources separate similar-looking hardwood and conifer species. For field use in Upper Michigan, this entry treats them as a wood-ear group and leans on substrate: hardwood logs and branches suggest an Auricularia angiospermarum-type wood ear, while conifer wood suggests an Auricularia americana-type wood ear.
records.notes: Brown ear-shaped to cup-shaped jelly fungi on dead wood; substrate matters because North American wood-ear taxonomy separates hardwood and conifer associates more than older field guides did.
records.general_notes: A field-useful wood-ear group entry. Record the host wood when possible: hardwood and conifer collections may represent different North American Auricularia species even when they look nearly identical.
records.field_identification: Look for thin brown to reddish-brown gelatinous ears, cups, or floppy lobes attached directly to dead wood. The outer surface may look finely hairy or frosted when fresh; the fertile surface is smoother and often wrinkled or veined. They dry dark and tough, then revive after rain. Note the host wood carefully, because substrate is one of the best field clues for separating North American wood-ear species.

underside: Lower / outer surface finely hairy and sterile
mushroom_profile.season_note: Spring through fall after wet weather; dried fruiting bodies can persist and revive after rain.
fertile_surface: Upper / inner smooth to wrinkled gelatinous surface

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Auricularia americana
use_links.notes: North American jelly-ear taxonomy, shape, substrate, and similar jelly fungi.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert wood-fungus page: Auricularia angiospermarum
use_links.notes: Hardwood wood-ear page and comparison with conifer-associated A. americana.

records.look_alike_notes: Brown jelly fungi such as Exidia species can look similar, but they usually form more blob-like, brain-like, or cushiony masses rather than thin ear-shaped cups. Leafy Tremella-like jelly fungi can form clustered folds, but they are usually more frilly or fused into masses instead of distinct ear-like lobes. Substrate and shape matter more here than color alone.

lookalike_risk.label: Moderate — jelly fungus / cup fungus confusion
lookalike_risk.short_label: Moderate — jelly fungus / cup fungus confusion
lookalike_risk.summary: Wood ear risk is mostly jelly fungus/cup fungus confusion; confirm ear-like gelatinous fruiting bodies attached to wood.
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: If it is not ear-like, gelatinous/rubbery, and wood-attached, do not call it wood ear.

target_traits: Ear-shaped rubbery-gelatinous lobe attached to wood
lookalike_traits: Blob-like or irregular jelly, less ear-shaped
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If it is not ear-like and wood-attached, do not call it wood ear.

lookalike_traits: Brittle or leathery cup on soil, not gelatinous wood ear
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Terrestrial brown cups should not be eaten as wood ear.
